findings.title: Unchecked low-level call return value
findings.description: Function `_wrap` has unchecked low-level call (line 7). The return value of `.call` / `.send` is not verified. If the call fails, execution continues silently. Capture and check: `(bool success,) = addr.call{...}(...); require(success);`

exploit_proof.verdict: LIKELY EXPLOITABLE: _wrap() — Z3 confirmed path feasibility but no invariant clearly violated.
attack_steps: 1. Target contract calls _wrap(malicious_address)
2. External .call() fails silently (no revert, no require on return)
3. Contract continues execution assuming success
4. State becomes inconsistent (balance recorded but not transferred, or vice versa)
5. INVARIANT BROKEN: no_free_money — accounting mismatch
exploit_proof.attack_profit: State inconsistency → potential fund drainage via follow-up

findings.title: Cross-function reentrancy
findings.description: Function `setTokenAdapter` makes external calls without reentrancy protection and shares state variables `_yieldTokens` with public function `setYieldTokenEnabled`. An attacker can re-enter via `setYieldTokenEnabled` during the external call and manipulate shared state. Apply nonReentrant modifier to both functions.

exploit_proof.verdict: LIKELY EXPLOITABLE: setYieldTokenEnabled() — Z3 confirmed path feasibility but no invariant clearly violated.
attack_steps: 1. Attacker deploys malicious contract with receive()/fallback()
2. Attacker deposits 0.000000 ETH → setYieldTokenEnabled() records balance
3. Attacker calls setYieldTokenEnabled()
4. Contract sends 0.000000 ETH via .call{value} BEFORE updating state
5. Attacker's receive() re-enters setYieldTokenEnabled()
6. require(balances[attacker] >= amount) PASSES — balance not yet decremented
7. Contract sends 0.000000 ETH AGAIN
8. State finally updates — but attacker already received 2x
9. INVARIANT BROKEN: balance_after < balance_before - withdraw_amount
10. Repeat steps 5-8 to drain entire contract
